examples.title: Basic Interrupt Handling Flow
examples.description: Pseudocode showing the sequence when CPU handles an interrupt request
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the complete interrupt handling sequence from receiving the interrupt request to returning to the main program, including state saving and restoration.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k = number of registers saved on stack

examples.title: Priority Encoder Simulation
examples.description: Python code simulating a 4-input priority encoder that determines highest priority interrupt
examples.code:
class PriorityEncoder:
    """4-input priority encoder with IRQ3 having highest priority"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.irqs = [False] * 4  # IRQ0 to IRQ3
        
    def set_interrupt(self, irq_num, value):
        """Set interrupt request line (0-3)"""
        if 0 <= irq_num <= 3:
            self.irqs[irq_num] = value
            
    def get_highest_priority(self):
        """Return (interrupt_number, binary_code) of highest priority active IRQ"""
        # Check from highest priority (IRQ3) to lowest (IRQ0)
        for i in range(3, -1, -1):
            if self.irqs[i]:
                return (i, i)  # interrupt number = binary code for 0-3
        return (-1, 0)  # No interrupt request
    
    def process_interrupts(self):
        """Process all pending interrupts in priority order"""
        handled = []
        while True:
            irq, code = self.get_highest_priority()
            if irq == -1:
                break
            # Simulate handling this interrupt
            handled.append(irq)
            self.irqs[irq] = False  # Clear the request
        return handled

# Demonstration
pe = PriorityEncoder()
pe.set_interrupt(0, True)   # Keyboard
pe.set_interrupt(2, True)   # Printer
pe.set_interrupt(3, True)  # Timer (highest)

print("IRQ Requests:", pe.irqs)
print("Highest Priority:", pe.get_highest_priority())
print("Handled in order:", pe.process_interrupts())
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates a priority encoder that handles multiple simultaneous interrupt requests, processing them in priority order (IRQ3 has highest priority, IRQ0 lowest).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of IRQ lines to check

examples.title: Interrupt Service Routine Registration
examples.description: C code demonstrating vector table setup and ISR registration for a hypothetical embedded system
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates how interrupt service routines are registered in a vector table (typical of ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers), showing the data structure, weak handlers, and functions to enable/configure interrupts.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for vector table size where n = number of interrupt sources

examples.title: Polling vs Interrupt Comparison
examples.description: Comparison algorithm showing both polling and interrupt approaches for handling device input
examples.explanation: This comparison demonstrates the fundamental difference between polling (continuous checking) and interrupt-driven I/O (event-driven), illustrating CPU utilization differences.
examples.timeComplexity: Polling: O(n×t) per cycle | Interrupt: O(t) only when interrupt occurs
examples.spaceComplexity: Both O(n) for device list storage
